quote: Whoever has my commandments and keeps them is the one who loves me.
firstReading: In Acts 14:5-18 Paul and Barnabas face violent opposition yet continue their mission. After Jews stir a crowd, Paul is stoned and dragged outside the city, presumed dead, but survives. When Paul heals a crippled man in Lystra the crowd mistakes the missionaries for gods, calling Barnabas Zeus and Paul Hermes. The apostles reject idolatrous honors, insist they are mere servants who proclaim the living God, and authenticate their message with signs and testimony.
psalmSummary: Psalm 115 contrasts the living God with powerless idols: give glory to the LORD rather than to ourselves, for his steadfast love and faithfulness endure. The psalm asks why nations should say, “Where is their God?” and answers that the Lord is in heaven doing all he wills. Idols are lifeless—mute, blind, helpless—while the faithful bless the LORD now and forever for his help and presence.
gospelSummary: In John 14:21-26 Jesus links love, obedience, and mutual indwelling. Whoever loves Jesus keeps his commandments; such love elicits the Father’s love and Jesus’ self-revelation. Jesus promises that those who love him will receive the Advocate—the Holy Spirit—who will teach them and bring to mind Jesus’ words. The Spirit proceeds from the Father in Jesus’ name and remains with the disciples, ensuring that the risen Lord’s instruction and presence continue within the believing community.
saintReflection: Though no specific saint is named today, the Acts reading calls us to follow the missionary courage of Paul and Barnabas. They resist flattery and idolatry, witness to the one God, and persist despite suffering. The saints model humility and steadfastness: they use signs to point beyond themselves to God, appoint leaders for the community, and place the mission of conversion above personal honor. Let their example inspire patient witness and fidelity to Christ’s commandments.
dailyPrayer: Lord Jesus, you who promised the Advocate and who reveal the Father to those who love you, grant us a listening heart. Teach us to keep your commandments as signs of our love, and send your Spirit to remind us of your words. When the world offers false honors or draws us toward idols, give us the courage of your apostles to point others to you. Strengthen our mission, renew our humility, and help us build communities guided by truth and charity. Make us faithful witnesses to your life, that your name alone may be glorified. Amen.
theologicalSynthesis: These readings intertwine mission, idolatry, obedience, and the Spirit. Acts shows how proclamation can provoke both healing and misunderstanding; signs authenticate the gospel yet must be used to direct honor to God alone. The psalm underscores the contrast between the living God and silent idols, grounding Christian witness in worship of the true Creator. John ties obedience to love: to keep Christ’s commandments is the fruit of love and the condition for deeper communion with Father and Son. The promised Advocate fulfills and preserves Jesus’ teaching, enabling the church’s memory and fidelity. Together they call the community to humble mission, sacramental assurance, and reliance on the Spirit rather than human acclaim.
exegesis:
Context: The Acts passage comes from Paul and Barnabas’ first missionary journey. They travel through hostile and hospitable cities, proclaiming the resurrection and calling people to repentance. In Lystra the dynamic shifts: a healing miracle elicits the city’s pagan interpretive frame and a misguided attempt to worship the healers as gods. The narrative contrasts human misunderstanding with apostolic purpose. Luke compresses conflict and confusion to show that gospel proclamation unsettles both civic and religious orders and that God’s advance through the apostles can provoke violence even as it authenticates divine power.

Psalm: Psalm 115 functions as a theological corrective to the Lystra scene. While the crowd mistakes Paul and Barnabas for Zeus and Hermes, the psalm stresses that idols are mute and impotent; only Yahweh acts decisively from heaven. The psalmist refuses human credit—“Not to us, O LORD, not to us”—and exhorts trust in God’s steadfast love. This contrast sharpens Luke’s point: the miraculous signs serve not to attract cultic praise to humans but to reveal and glorify the living God who alone acts on behalf of his people.

Gospel: John 14:21-26 frames Christian life around love, obedience, and the Spirit’s continuing presence. Love for Jesus is concretely shown by keeping his commandments; that obedience is not legalistic but relational, the natural response of one who belongs to Christ. The Father’s love and Jesus’ self-revelation follow such fidelity, indicating mutual indwelling: love establishes a communion in which God’s presence is manifest. The Spirit—called Advocate, Teacher, and Remembrancer—ensures that Jesus’ words remain formative for the community. The promise of the Spirit shifts the center of authority: memory and teaching become gifts of the Spirit, safeguarding apostolic instruction beyond the earthly ministry of Jesus.

Saints: Even without a named feast, the Acts narrative itself offers models of sanctity. Paul and Barnabas embody apostolic holiness: bold proclamation, readiness to suffer, and refusal of misplaced honor. Their tearing of garments and insistence that they are servants of the living God is a teachable moment about humility and pastoral integrity. Saints live by pointing away from themselves to God, and their signs function as authenticating pointers rather than ends in themselves. The apostolic practice of strengthening disciples and appointing elders also shows that sanctity entails pastoral responsibility—building durable communities under God’s rule.

Today: The readings address contemporary temptations. Modern idols may be not bronze statues but ideologies, celebrity, success, or technological power—forces that claim ultimate loyalty. The psalm and Acts together call Christians to witness that God alone gives life and accomplishes wonders. John’s promise comforts the church: obedience expressed in love is accompanied by the Spirit’s teaching presence, preventing the community from losing Jesus’ message or mistaking human acclaim for divine endorsement. Pastoral leaders must emulate the apostles by cultivating humility, discipling others, and forming communities where memory of Christ is kept alive by the Spirit.

Conclusion: Bringing these threads together, the passage invites a balanced Christian response: witness with signs when necessary, but always refuse idolatrous praise; embody commandments as responsive love; and rely on the Spirit to teach, remind, and preserve the church’s fidelity. The early missionaries teach that suffering and misunderstanding can accompany proclamation, yet the living God vindicates his message through enduring love and the Spirit’s abiding presence. For the believer, the task is simple but demanding: love Christ by keeping his word, trust the Lord alone, and let the Advocate form the community into his faithful witness.
